skill.name: uv-package-manager
skill.description: Guide for using uv to create Python projects, manage dependencies, run virtual environments, lock packages, and migrate from pip or Poetry workflows.
skill.summary: Use uv for faster Python dependency, environment, lockfile, and project workflows.
skill.author: ActiveInferenceInstitute

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, network, and filesystem patterns because this is a command-heavy uv package-manager guide. AI review found no evidence of malicious intent, data exfiltration, prompt injection, hidden persistence, or dynamic command injection. The main confirmed risks are documented remote installer commands and shell configuration examples, so publication is acceptable with warnings.

medium_findings.title: Remote Installer Piped To Shell
medium_findings.description: The install section recommends running a downloaded shell script directly with sh. This is a known risky installation pattern because a compromised endpoint, network path, or copied command can execute arbitrary code on the user system. Context indicates this is the official uv installer guidance, so it is a legitimate but dangerous pattern rather than evidence of malicious intent.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact pipe-to-shell command is present and would execute remote code if run. Confidence is high about the risk pattern, while the surrounding installation context supports legitimate intent.

medium_findings.title: PowerShell Remote Script Execution
medium_findings.description: The Windows install example downloads a PowerShell installer and executes it through iex. This can execute arbitrary remote code and should be treated with caution, even though the URL points to the uv project installer.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command directly invokes a remote PowerShell script with iex. The usage appears to be installer documentation, not hidden persistence or exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Many External Command Examples
medium_findings.description: The file contains many uv, pip, git, Docker, shell, and CI examples. These are expected for a uv package-manager skill and do not show command injection, dynamic command construction, or concealed execution by the skill itself.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible documentation examples and mostly use fixed arguments. Confidence is high that the broad static matches are expected, but users should still review commands before running them.

low_findings.title: Home Directory Configuration Example
low_findings.description: The troubleshooting section shows appending a PATH export to ~/.bashrc. This modifies a user shell startup file, but it is clearly documented as a PATH repair step and not hidden persistence.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line writes to a shell configuration file, which is sensitive. The context is transparent troubleshooting, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Official Project URLs
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged hardcoded URLs. The observed URLs point to uv official documentation, the Astral site, and the uv GitHub repository, with no evidence of credential exfiltration or suspicious endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The links are visible resource references for the documented tool. No evidence found of outbound data transfer or hidden network behavior.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Finding Is A False Positive
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections appear to match ordinary text or URL content, not cryptographic code. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, or other weak algorithms being used for security decisions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed flagged lines do not contain cryptographic implementation or security checks. The scanner likely matched substrings in prose or URLs.

This is a known risky installation pattern because a compromised endpoint, network path, or copied command can execute arbitrary code on the user system. content.value_statement: Python dependency workflows can be slow and inconsistent across local, CI, and Docker environments. This skill gives practical uv commands for faster installs, lockfiles, virtual environments, and migration paths.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to install uv on macOS, Linux, Windows, pip, Homebrew, and cargo.
Shows how to initialize Python projects, add dependencies, remove packages, and sync environments.
Covers virtual environment creation, activation, uv run workflows, and Python version pinning.
Provides examples for lockfiles, CI pipelines, Docker builds, workspaces, and offline installs.
Gives migration guidance from pip requirements files, Poetry projects, and pip-tools workflows.
Lists troubleshooting commands for PATH issues, cache cleanup, lockfile sync, and dependency conflicts.
limitations: It is documentation only and does not run uv commands automatically.
Some installer examples execute remote scripts and require user verification before use.
Version details may change as uv evolves, so users should confirm current uv documentation.
It focuses on Python package workflows and does not replace full Python packaging design review.

use_cases.title: Start a Python service quickly
use_cases.description: Create a new uv project, pin Python, add runtime packages, add test tools, and run commands through uv run.

use_cases.title: Standardize CI dependency installs
use_cases.description: Use uv lockfiles, frozen sync, setup-uv caching, and repeatable test commands in continuous integration.

use_cases.title: Move legacy dependency files to uv
use_cases.description: Convert requirements, Poetry, or pip-tools workflows into uv sync, uv add, and uv lock patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Create a new uv project
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a new Python project with uv. Include project initialization, Python version pinning, runtime dependencies, dev dependencies, and the commands I should run.

prompt_templates.title: Add uv to an existing project
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my current Python dependency workflow and give me a uv migration plan. Include how to handle pyproject.toml, requirements files, virtual environments, and lockfiles.

prompt_templates.title: Design a CI workflow with uv
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a CI dependency workflow using uv. Include installation, Python setup, cache use, frozen lockfile sync, tests, linting, and failure cases to watch for.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize a container build with uv
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an optimized Docker build for a Python app using uv. Include lockfile handling, dependency layer caching, production-only installs, and runtime environment setup.

output_examples.input: I need a fast setup for a new FastAPI project.
output: A project initialization sequence using uv init and Python pinning.
Runtime dependency choices for FastAPI and uvicorn.
Development tools for tests, formatting, linting, and type checking.
A repeatable uv run workflow for local commands.

output_examples.input: My CI is slow because pip installs dependencies every run.
output: A uv-based CI outline with setup-uv caching.
Frozen sync from the lockfile for repeatable builds.
Separate test and lint commands executed through uv run.
Notes about committing uv.lock and cache configuration.

output_examples.input: I have requirements.txt and want to adopt uv safely.
output: A migration path from requirements.txt to pyproject.toml.
Commands for installing existing requirements and creating a lockfile.
Advice to verify dependency resolution before removing old files.

best_practices: Review remote installer commands before running them, especially pipe-to-shell and PowerShell iex examples.
Commit uv.lock and use frozen sync in CI for repeatable dependency installs.
Use uv run for project commands so tools use the intended virtual environment.
anti_patterns: Do not run copied installer commands without confirming the source and current project documentation.
Do not edit shell startup files blindly when fixing PATH issues.
Do not regenerate lockfiles in CI when builds should be reproducible.

faq.question: Does this skill run uv commands for me?
faq.answer: No. It provides command guidance and workflow examples that you can review and run yourself.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks compatibility with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Is uv a replacement for pip?
faq.answer: It can replace many pip workflows and also supports project, virtual environment, lockfile, and Python version tasks.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill includes remote installer commands and shell configuration examples that are legitimate but risky if copied without review.

faq.question: Does the skill include Docker and CI examples?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes GitHub Actions, Docker, lockfile, cache, and frozen install workflow examples.

faq.question: Should I verify uv syntax before using the examples?
faq.answer: Yes. uv changes quickly, so confirm commands against the current official uv documentation for production use.
